infobox.value: Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:IPA#Rendering_issues "Help:IPA"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") characters. For an introductory guide on IPA symbols, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). | |

(മലയാളം, *Malayāḷam*, [[mɐlɐjaːɭɐm]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Malayalam "Help:IPA/Malayalam") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Malayalam.ogg "File:Malayalam.ogg"))[[a]](#cite_note-10) is a [Dravidian language](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages"), primarily spoken by the [Malayali people](/wiki/Malayalis "Malayalis"), native to the [Indian](/wiki/India "India") state of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") and the union territories of [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep") and [Puducherry](/wiki/Puducherry_(union_territory) "Puducherry (union territory)") ([Mahé district](/wiki/Mah%C3%A9_district "Mahé district")). It is one of 22 [scheduled languages](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India"), as well as one of 11 [classical languages](/wiki/Classical_Languages_of_India "Classical Languages of India"), of India.[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12) Malayalam has [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") status in Kerala, Lakshadweep and Puducherry ([Mahé](/wiki/Mah%C3%A9,_Puducherry "Mahé, Puducherry")).[[2]](#cite_note-kerala.gov.in-2)[[3]](#cite_note-P&ARD_Official_Languages-3)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Malayalam.svg/40px-Malayalam.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Malayalam.svg)

Malayalam is written in a [non-Latin script](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script"). Malayalam text used in this article is transliterated into the Latin script according to the [ISO 15919](/wiki/ISO_15919 "ISO 15919") standard.

A Malayalam speaker, recorded in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa")

**Malayalam** (മലയാളം, *Malayāḷam*, [[mɐlɐjaːɭɐm]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Malayalam "Help:IPA/Malayalam") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Malayalam.ogg "File:Malayalam.ogg"))[[a]](#cite_note-10) is a [Dravidian language](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages"), primarily spoken by the [Malayali people](/wiki/Malayalis "Malayalis"), native to the [Indian](/wiki/India "India") state of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") and the union territories of [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep") and [Puducherry](/wiki/Puducherry_(union_territory) "Puducherry (union territory)") ([Mahé district](/wiki/Mah%C3%A9_district "Mahé district")). It is one of 22 [scheduled languages](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India"), as well as one of 11 [classical languages](/wiki/Classical_Languages_of_India "Classical Languages of India"), of India.[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12) Malayalam has [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") status in Kerala, Lakshadweep and Puducherry ([Mahé](/wiki/Mah%C3%A9,_Puducherry "Mahé, Puducherry")).[[2]](#cite_note-kerala.gov.in-2)[[3]](#cite_note-P&ARD_Official_Languages-3)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

Malayalam is spoken by significant numbers of people in India[[13]](#cite_note-14) and is the majority language in the state of Kerala. Malayalam is also spoken by linguistic minorities in the neighbouring states; with a significant number of speakers in the [Kodagu](/wiki/Kodagu "Kodagu") and [Dakshina Kannada](/wiki/Dakshina_Kannada "Dakshina Kannada") districts of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), and [Kanyakumari](/wiki/Kanyakumari_district "Kanyakumari district"), [Coimbatore](/wiki/Coimbatore_district "Coimbatore district") and [Nilgiris](/wiki/Nilgiris_district "Nilgiris district") district of Tamil Nadu. It is also spoken by the [Malayali Diaspora](/wiki/Malayali_diaspora "Malayali diaspora") worldwide, especially in the [Persian Gulf countries](/wiki/Arab_states_of_the_Persian_Gulf "Arab states of the Persian Gulf"), due to the large populations of [Malayali](/wiki/Malayali "Malayali") expatriates there. They are a significant population in each city in [India](/wiki/India "India") including [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai_Metropolitan_Region "Mumbai Metropolitan Region"), [Bengaluru](/wiki/Bengaluru "Bengaluru"), [Chennai](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai"), [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), [Hyderabad](/wiki/Hyderabad "Hyderabad") etc.

The origin of Malayalam remains a matter of dispute among scholars. The mainstream view holds that Malayalam descends from a western coastal dialect of early [Middle Tamil](/wiki/Middle_Tamil "Middle Tamil") and separated from it sometime between the 9th and 13th centuries,[[14]](#cite_note-ayyar-1936-15)[[15]](#cite_note-krishnamurti-2003-16) although this medieval western dialect also preserved some archaisms suggesting an earlier divergence of the spoken dialects in the prehistoric period. A second view argues for the development of the two languages out of "Proto-Dravidian" or "Proto-Tamil-Malayalam" either in the prehistoric period or in the middle of the first millennium A.D.,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherKumari1997xxiv-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKarashima2014p._6-19) although this is generally rejected by historical linguists.[[19]](#cite_note-20) The [Quilon Syrian copper plates](/wiki/Quilon_Syrian_copper_plates "Quilon Syrian copper plates") of 849/850 CE are considered by some to be the oldest available inscription written in [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam"). However, the existence of Old Malayalam is sometimes disputed by scholars.[[20]](#cite_note-freeman-2003-21) They regard the [Chera Perumal](/wiki/Chera_Perumals_of_Makotai "Chera Perumals of Makotai") inscriptional language as a diverging dialect or variety of contemporary [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language").[[20]](#cite_note-freeman-2003-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22) The oldest extant literary work in Malayalam distinct from the Tamil tradition is *[Ramacharitam](/wiki/Ramacharitam "Ramacharitam")* (late 12th or early 13th century).[[22]](#cite_note-23)

The earliest script used to write Malayalam was the [Vatteluttu script](/wiki/Vatteluttu_alphabet "Vatteluttu alphabet").[[15]](#cite_note-krishnamurti-2003-16) The current [Malayalam script](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script") is based on the Vatteluttu script, which was extended with [Grantha script](/wiki/Grantha_script "Grantha script") letters to adopt [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") loanwords.[[15]](#cite_note-krishnamurti-2003-16)[[23]](#cite_note-24) It bears high similarity with the [Tigalari script](/wiki/Tigalari_script "Tigalari script"), a historical script that was used to write the [Tulu language](/wiki/Tulu_language "Tulu language") in [South Canara](/wiki/South_Canara "South Canara"), and [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") in the adjacent [Malabar region](/wiki/Malabar_District "Malabar District").[[24]](#cite_note-vinodh_rajan-25) The modern Malayalam grammar is based on the book *[Kerala Panineeyam](/wiki/Kerala_Panineeyam "Kerala Panineeyam")* written by [A. R. Raja Raja Varma](/wiki/A._R._Raja_Raja_Varma "A. R. Raja Raja Varma") in late 19th century CE.[[25]](#cite_note-Chandran_2018-26) The first travelogue in any Indian language is the Malayalam *[Varthamanappusthakam](/wiki/Varthamanappusthakam "Varthamanappusthakam")*, written by [Paremmakkal Thoma Kathanar](/wiki/Paremmakkal_Thoma_Kathanar "Paremmakkal Thoma Kathanar") in 1785.[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28)

[Robert Caldwell](/wiki/Robert_Caldwell "Robert Caldwell") describes the extent of Malayalam in the 19th century as extending from the vicinity of [Kumbla](/wiki/Kumbla "Kumbla") in the north where it supersedes with [Tulu](/wiki/Tulu_language "Tulu language") to [Kanyakumari](/wiki/Kanyakumari "Kanyakumari") in the south, where it begins to be superseded by [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"),[[28]](#cite_note-29) beside the inhabited islands of [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep") in the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea").

## Etymology

In a 7th-century poem written by the Tamil poet [Sambandar](/wiki/Sambandar "Sambandar") the people of Kerala are referred to as *malaiyāḷar* (mountain people).[[29]](#cite_note-30) The word *Malayalam* is also said to originate from the words *mala*, meaning '[mountain](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain")', and *alam*, meaning '[region](/wiki/Region "Region")' or '-ship' (as in "township"); *Malayalam* thus translates directly as 'the [mountain](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain") region'. The term *[Malabar](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast")* was used as an alternative term for *Malayalam* in foreign trade circles to denote the southwestern coast of the Indian peninsula, which also means *The land of hills*.[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) The term originally referred to the western hilly [land](/wiki/Land "Land") of the [Chera dynasty](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty") (later [Zamorins](/wiki/Zamorin "Zamorin") and the [Kingdom of Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin")), [Kingdom of Ezhimala](/wiki/Mushika_dynasty "Mushika dynasty") (later [Kolathunadu](/wiki/Kolathunadu "Kolathunadu")), and [Ay kingdom](/wiki/Ay_kingdom "Ay kingdom") (later [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore")), and only later became the name of its language.[[34]](#cite_note-caldwell-35) The language Malayalam was alternatively called *Alealum*, *Malayalani*, *Malayali*, *Malabari*, *Malean*, *Maliyad*, *Mallealle*, and *Kerala Bhasha* until the early 19th century CE.[[35]](#cite_note-ethnologuecom-36)[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

The earliest extant [literary](/wiki/Literary "Literary") works in the regional [language](/wiki/Language "Language") of present-day [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") probably date back to as early as the [12th century](/wiki/12th_century "12th century"). At that time, the language was differentiated by the name *Kerala Bhasha*. The earliest mention of Malayalam as a language is found outside of Kerala in the 15th century [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language") work Śrībhīmēśvarapurāṇamu by Śrīnātha.[[38]](#cite_note-39) The distinctive "Malayalam" named identity of this [language](/wiki/Language "Language") appears to have come into existence in Kerala only around the [16th century](/wiki/16th_century "16th century"), when it was known as "Malayayma" or "Malayanma"; the words were also used to refer to the [script](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") and the [region](/wiki/Region "Region").[[39]](#cite_note-Raghunathan_2003-40) According to [Duarte Barbosa](/wiki/Duarte_Barbosa "Duarte Barbosa"), a Portuguese visitor who visited Kerala in the early 16th century CE, the people in the southwestern [Malabar coast](/wiki/Malabar_coast "Malabar coast") of India from [Kumbla](/wiki/Kumbla "Kumbla") in north to [Kanyakumari](/wiki/Kanyakumari "Kanyakumari") in south had a unique language, which was called "Maliama" by them.[[40]](#cite_note-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42)

Prior to this [period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"), the people of Kerala usually referred to their language as "Tamil", and both terms overlapped into the [colonial period](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").[[note 1]](#cite_note-43)

## History

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Quilon_Syrian_copper_plates_%28849_AD%29.jpg/250px-Quilon_Syrian_copper_plates_%28849_AD%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quilon_Syrian_copper_plates_(849_AD).jpg)

The [Quilon Syrian copper plates](/wiki/Quilon_Syrian_copper_plates "Quilon Syrian copper plates") (849/850 CE) are considered as the oldest available inscription written in [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam").[[42]](#cite_note-44) Besides [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam"), the copper plate also contains signatures in [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") (Kufic script), [Middle Persian](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian") (cursive Pahlavi script) and [Judeo-Persian](/wiki/Judeo-Persian "Judeo-Persian") (standard square [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language")) scripts.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

Due to the geographical isolation of the [Malabar Coast](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast") from the rest of the [Indian peninsula](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") due to the presence of the [Western Ghats](/wiki/Western_Ghats "Western Ghats") mountain ranges which lie parallel to the coast, the dialect of [Old Tamil](/wiki/Old_Tamil "Old Tamil") spoken in [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") was different from that spoken in [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu").[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37) The mainstream view holds that Malayalam began to grow as a distinct literary language from the western coastal dialect of [Middle Tamil](/wiki/Middle_Tamil "Middle Tamil")[[44]](#cite_note-46) and the linguistic separation completed sometime between the 9th and 13th centuries.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKarashima2014p._6-19)[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGopinathan_Nair2009682-47) The renowned poets of [Classical Tamil](/wiki/Old_Tamil "Old Tamil") such as [Paranar](/wiki/Paranar "Paranar") (1st century CE), [Ilango Adigal](/wiki/Ilango_Adigal "Ilango Adigal") (2nd–3rd century CE), and [Kulasekhara Alvar](/wiki/Kulasekhara_Alvar "Kulasekhara Alvar") (9th century CE) were [Keralites](/wiki/Malayali "Malayali").[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37) The [Sangam works](/wiki/Sangam_literature "Sangam literature") can be considered as the ancient predecessor of Malayalam.[[46]](#cite_note-Chandran_2018a-48)

According to Govindankutty, both [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") and Malayalam developed during the prehistoric period from a common ancestor, "Proto-Tamil-Malayalam", and that the notion of Malayalam being a "daughter" of [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") is misplaced.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherKumari1997xxiv-17) This is based on the fact that Malayalam and several [Dravidian languages](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages") on the [Western Coast](/wiki/Western_Coastal_Plains "Western Coastal Plains") have common archaic features which are not found even in the oldest historical forms of literary Tamil.[[47]](#cite_note-49) Despite this, Malayalam shares many common innovations with Tamil that emerged during the early [Middle Tamil](/wiki/Middle_Tamil "Middle Tamil") period, thus making independent descent untenable.[[14]](#cite_note-ayyar-1936-15)[[note 2]](#cite_note-51)
Indeed, most features of Malayalam morphology are derivable from a form of speech corresponding to early Middle Tamil.[[49]](#cite_note-52)

[Robert Caldwell](/wiki/Robert_Caldwell "Robert Caldwell"), in his 1856 book "*A Comparative Grammar of the Dravidian or South-Indian Family of Languages"*, opined that literary Malayalam branched from *Classical Tamil* and over time gained a large amount of [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") vocabulary and lost the personal terminations of verbs.[[34]](#cite_note-caldwell-35) As the language of scholarship and administration, Old-Tamil, which was written in [Tamil-Brahmi](/wiki/Tamil-Brahmi "Tamil-Brahmi") and the Vatteluttu alphabet later, greatly influenced the early development of Malayalam as a literary language. The [Malayalam script](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script") began to diverge from the *[Vatteluttu](/wiki/Vatteluttu "Vatteluttu")* and the Western [Grantha](/wiki/Grantha_script "Grantha script") scripts in the 8th and 9th centuries of [Common Era](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era"). By the end of the 13th century, a written form of the language emerged which was unique from the *[Vatteluttu](/wiki/Vatteluttu "Vatteluttu")* script that was used to write Tamil on the eastern coast.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMahapatra1989307-53)

### Old Malayalam

[Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam") (*Paḻaya Malayāḷam*), an inscriptional language found in [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") from the 9th to the 13th century CE,[[51]](#cite_note-54) is the earliest attested form of Malayalam.[[52]](#cite_note-narayanan-2013-55)[[53]](#cite_note-56) The development of [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam") from a western coastal dialect of [Middle Tamil](/wiki/Middle_Tamil "Middle Tamil") began around the 8th century CE.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKarashima2014152–153-57)[[15]](#cite_note-krishnamurti-2003-16)[[55]](#cite_note-58) It remained a west coast dialect until circa 9th century CE or a little later.[[56]](#cite_note-krishnamurti-59)[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKarashima2014152–153-57) The origin of [Malayalam calendar](/wiki/Malayalam_calendar "Malayalam calendar") dates back to year 825 CE.[[57]](#cite_note-60)[[58]](#cite_note-61)[[59]](#cite_note-62) It is generally agreed that the western coastal dialect of Tamil began to separate, diverge, and grow as a distinct language due to geographical separation of Kerala from the Tamil country[[56]](#cite_note-krishnamurti-59) and the influence of [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") and [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit") from the [Nambudiri](/wiki/Nambudiri "Nambudiri") [Brahmins](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") of the [Malabar Coast](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast").[[52]](#cite_note-narayanan-2013-55)[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37)

The [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam") language was employed in several official records and transactions (at the level of the [Chera Perumal](/wiki/Chera_Perumals_of_Makotai "Chera Perumals of Makotai") kings, as well as the [upper-caste](/wiki/Caste "Caste") ([Nambudiri](/wiki/Nambudiri "Nambudiri")) village temples).[[52]](#cite_note-narayanan-2013-55) Most of the inscriptions in [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam") were found from the [northern districts of Kerala](/wiki/List_of_districts_of_Kerala "List of districts of Kerala"), which lie adjacent to [Tulu Nadu](/wiki/Tulu_Nadu "Tulu Nadu").[[52]](#cite_note-narayanan-2013-55) [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam") was mostly written in [Vatteluttu](/wiki/Vatteluttu "Vatteluttu") script (with [Pallava/Southern Grantha](/wiki/Grantha_script "Grantha script") characters).[[52]](#cite_note-narayanan-2013-55) [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam") had several features distinct from the contemporary Tamil, including the nasalisation of adjoining sounds, substitution of palatal sounds for dental sounds, contraction of vowels, and the rejection of gender verbs.[[52]](#cite_note-narayanan-2013-55)[[60]](#cite_note-63)[[61]](#cite_note-64) *[Ramacharitam](/wiki/Ramacharitam "Ramacharitam")* and *[Thirunizhalmala](/wiki/Thirunizhalmala "Thirunizhalmala")* are the possible literary works of [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam") found so far.

### Middle Malayalam

[Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam") gradually developed into [Middle Malayalam](/wiki/Middle_Malayalam "Middle Malayalam") (*Madhyakaala Malayalam*) by the 13th century CE.[[62]](#cite_note-65) [Malayalam literature](/wiki/Malayalam_literature "Malayalam literature") also completely diverged from [Tamil literature](/wiki/Tamil_literature "Tamil literature") during this period. Works including *Unniyachi Charitham*, *Unnichiruthevi Charitham*, and *Unniyadi Charitham*, are written in [Middle Malayalam](/wiki/Middle_Malayalam "Middle Malayalam"), and date back to the 13th and 14th centuries of the [Common Era](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era").[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66)[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37) The *Sandesha Kavya*s of 14th century CE written in [Manipravalam](/wiki/Manipravalam "Manipravalam") language include *[Unnuneeli Sandesam](/wiki/Unnuneeli_Sandesam "Unnuneeli Sandesam")*.[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66)[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37) *Kannassa Ramayanam* and *Kannassa Bharatham* by *Rama Panikkar* of the [Niranam poets](/wiki/Niranam_poets "Niranam poets") who lived between 1350 and 1450, are representative of this language.[[64]](#cite_note-kerala_india-2003-67) [Ulloor](/wiki/Ulloor_S._Parameswara_Iyer "Ulloor S. Parameswara Iyer") has opined that Rama Panikkar holds the same position in [Malayalam literature](/wiki/Malayalam_literature "Malayalam literature") that [Edmund Spenser](/wiki/Edmund_Spenser "Edmund Spenser") does in [English literature](/wiki/English_literature "English literature").[[64]](#cite_note-kerala_india-2003-67) The *[Champu](/wiki/Champu "Champu") Kavyas* written by Punam Nambudiri, one among the *Pathinettara Kavikal* (Eighteen and a half poets) in the court of the [Zamorin of Calicut](/wiki/Zamorin_of_Calicut "Zamorin of Calicut"), also belong to Middle Malayalam.[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37)[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66) The literary works of this period were heavily influenced by [Manipravalam](/wiki/Manipravalam "Manipravalam"), which was a combination of contemporary [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") and [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit").[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37) The word *Mani-Pravalam* literally means *Diamond-Coral* or *Ruby-Coral*. The 14th-century *[Lilatilakam](/wiki/Lilatilakam "Lilatilakam")* text states Manipravalam to be a *Bhashya* (language) where "Dravida and Sanskrit should combine together like ruby and coral, without the least trace of any discord".[[65]](#cite_note-arvind_raghunathan-2003-68)[[66]](#cite_note-ke_rāmacandr̲an_nāyar-69) The scripts of *[Kolezhuthu](/wiki/Kolezhuthu "Kolezhuthu")* and *[Malayanma](/wiki/Malayanma "Malayanma")* were also used to write [Middle Malayalam](/wiki/Middle_Malayalam "Middle Malayalam"). In addition to *Vatteluthu* and [Grantha script](/wiki/Grantha_script "Grantha script"), those were used to write [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam").[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37) The literary works written in [Middle Malayalam](/wiki/Middle_Malayalam "Middle Malayalam") were heavily influenced by [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") and [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit"), while comparing them with the modern [Malayalam literature](/wiki/Malayalam_literature "Malayalam literature").[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66)[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Copy_of_Ezhuthachan%27s_Adhyathma_ramayanam_Kilippattu.jpg/330px-Copy_of_Ezhuthachan%27s_Adhyathma_ramayanam_Kilippattu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Copy_of_Ezhuthachan%27s_Adhyathma_ramayanam_Kilippattu.jpg)

Copy of Ezhuthachan's [stylus](/wiki/Stylus "Stylus") and *[Adhyatma Ramayanam](/wiki/Adhyathmaramayanam "Adhyathmaramayanam")* preserved at [Thunchan Parambu, Tirur](/wiki/Thunchan_Parambu,_Tirur "Thunchan Parambu, Tirur")

### Modern Malayalam

The [Middle Malayalam](/wiki/Middle_Malayalam "Middle Malayalam") was succeeded by Modern Malayalam (*Aadhunika Malayalam*) by 15th century CE.[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37) The poem *[Krishnagatha](/wiki/Krishnagatha "Krishnagatha")* written by [Cherusseri Namboothiri](/wiki/Cherusseri_Namboothiri "Cherusseri Namboothiri"), who was the court poet of the king Udaya Varman Kolathiri (1446–1475) of [Kolathunadu](/wiki/Kolathunadu "Kolathunadu"), is written in modern Malayalam.[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66) The language used in *Krishnagatha* is the modern spoken form of Malayalam.[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66) During the 16th century CE, [Thunchaththu Ezhuthachan](/wiki/Thunchaththu_Ezhuthachan "Thunchaththu Ezhuthachan") from the [Kingdom of Tanur](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Tanur "Kingdom of Tanur") and [Poonthanam Nambudiri](/wiki/Poonthanam_Nambudiri "Poonthanam Nambudiri") from the [Kingdom of Valluvanad](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Valluvanad "Kingdom of Valluvanad"), followed the new trend initiated by Cherussery in their poems. The *[Adhyathmaramayanam Kilippattu](/wiki/Adhyathmaramayanam_Kilippattu "Adhyathmaramayanam Kilippattu")* and *Mahabharatham [Kilippattu](/wiki/Kilippattu "Kilippattu"),* written by Ezhuthachan, and *[Jnanappana](/wiki/Jnanappana "Jnanappana"),* written by Poonthanam, are also included in the earliest form of Modern Malayalam.[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66)

Comparison of [Grantha](/wiki/Grantha_script "Grantha script"), [Tigalari](/wiki/Tigalari_script "Tigalari script"), and [Malayalam scripts](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script")

|  | Letter | | | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Script | ka | kha | ga | gha | ṅa |
| Malayalam | ക | ഖ | ഗ | ഘ | ങ |
| Grantha | 𑌕 | 𑌖 | 𑌗 | 𑌘 | 𑌙 |
| Tigalari | 𑎒 | 𑎓 | 𑎔 | 𑎕 | 𑎖 |
| Tamil | க |  |  |  | ங |
| Sinhala | ක් | ඛ | ග | ඝ | ණ |
| Kannada | ಕ | ಖ | ಗ | ಘ | ಙ |

[Thunchaththu Ezhuthachan](/wiki/Thunchaththu_Ezhuthachan "Thunchaththu Ezhuthachan") is also credited with developing the [Malayalam script](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script") into the current form through the intermixing and modification of the erstwhile scripts of *[Vatteluttu](/wiki/Vatteluttu "Vatteluttu")*, *[Kolezhuthu](/wiki/Kolezhuthu "Kolezhuthu")*, and [Grantha script](/wiki/Grantha_script "Grantha script"), which were used to write the inscriptions and literary works of Old and Middle Malayalam.[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66) He further eliminated excess and unnecessary letters from the modified script.[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66) Hence, Ezhuthachan is also known as *The Father of modern Malayalam*.[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66) The development of modern [Malayalam script](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script") was also heavily influenced by the [Tigalari script](/wiki/Tigalari_script "Tigalari script"), which was used to write [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), due to the influence of [Tuluva Brahmins](/wiki/Tuluva_Brahmin "Tuluva Brahmin") in Kerala.[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66) The language used in the [Arabi Malayalam](/wiki/Arabi_Malayalam "Arabi Malayalam") works of the 16th–17th century CE is a mixture of Modern Malayalam and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language").[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66) They follow the syntax of modern Malayalam, though written in a modified form of [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script"), which is known as [Arabi Malayalam script](/wiki/Arabi_Malayalam_script "Arabi Malayalam script").[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66) P. Shangunny Menon ascribes the authorship of the medieval work *[Keralolpathi](/wiki/Keralolpathi "Keralolpathi")*, which describes the [Parashurama](/wiki/Parashurama "Parashurama") legend and the departure of the final Cheraman Perumal king to [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"), to Thunchaththu Ramanujan Ezhuthachan.[[67]](#cite_note-history_of_travancore_by_shungunny_menon-70)

[Kunchan Nambiar](/wiki/Kunchan_Nambiar "Kunchan Nambiar") introduced a new literary form called *Thullal*, and [Unnayi Variyar](/wiki/Unnayi_Variyar "Unnayi Variyar") introduced reforms in *[Attakkatha literature](/wiki/Kathakali "Kathakali")*.[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66) The printing, prose literature, and [Malayalam journalism](/wiki/Malayalam_journalism "Malayalam journalism"), developed after the latter-half of the 18th century CE. Modern literary movements in Malayalam literature began in the late 19th century with the rise of the famous Modern Triumvirate consisting of [Kumaran Asan](/wiki/Kumaran_Asan "Kumaran Asan"),[[68]](#cite_note-71) [Ulloor S. Parameswara Iyer](/wiki/Ulloor_S._Parameswara_Iyer "Ulloor S. Parameswara Iyer")[[69]](#cite_note-72) and [Vallathol Narayana Menon](/wiki/Vallathol_Narayana_Menon "Vallathol Narayana Menon").[[70]](#cite_note-73) In the second half of the 20th century, [Jnanpith](/wiki/Jnanpith "Jnanpith") winning poets and writers like [G. Sankara Kurup](/wiki/G._Sankara_Kurup "G. Sankara Kurup"), [S. K. Pottekkatt](/wiki/S._K._Pottekkatt "S. K. Pottekkatt"), [Thakazhi Sivasankara Pillai](/wiki/Thakazhi_Sivasankara_Pillai "Thakazhi Sivasankara Pillai"), [M. T. Vasudevan Nair](/wiki/M._T._Vasudevan_Nair "M. T. Vasudevan Nair"), [O. N. V. Kurup](/wiki/O._N._V._Kurup "O. N. V. Kurup"), and [Akkitham Achuthan Namboothiri](/wiki/Akkitham_Achuthan_Namboothiri "Akkitham Achuthan Namboothiri"), had made valuable contributions to the modern Malayalam literature.[[71]](#cite_note-subodh_kapoor-2002-74)[[72]](#cite_note-E.G._Smith_1994-75)[[73]](#cite_note-nirmala_sadanand_publishers-1967-76)[[74]](#cite_note-sahitya_akademi-1987-77)[[75]](#cite_note-kerala_sahitya_akademi-1993-78) The life and works of [Edasseri Govindan Nair](/wiki/Edasseri_Govindan_Nair "Edasseri Govindan Nair") have assumed greater socio-literary significance after his death and Edasseri is now recognised as an important poet of Malayalam.[[76]](#cite_note-79) Later, writers like [O. V. Vijayan](/wiki/O._V._Vijayan "O. V. Vijayan"), [Kamaladas](/wiki/Kamaladas "Kamaladas"), [M. Mukundan](/wiki/M._Mukundan "M. Mukundan"), [Arundhati Roy](/wiki/Arundhati_Roy "Arundhati Roy"), and [Vaikom Muhammed Basheer](/wiki/Vaikom_Muhammed_Basheer "Vaikom Muhammed Basheer"), have gained international recognition.[[77]](#cite_note-c_gopinathan_pillai-2004-80)[[78]](#cite_note-ed_vinod_kumar_maheshwari-2002-81)[[79]](#cite_note-amit_chaudhuri-2008-82) Malayalam has also borrowed a lot of its words from various foreign languages: mainly from the [Semitic languages](/wiki/Semitic_languages "Semitic languages") including [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), and the [European languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Europe "Languages of Europe") including [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") and [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), due to the long heritage of [Indian Ocean trade](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_trade "Indian Ocean trade") and the Portuguese-Dutch colonization of the [Malabar Coast](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast").[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37)[[63]](#cite_note-dr_k_ayyappa_panicker-2006-66)

Malayalam is taught in public schools of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"),Lakshadweep and [Puducherry](/wiki/Puducherry_(union_territory) "Puducherry (union territory)") ([Mahé](/wiki/Mah%C3%A9_district "Mahé district")) (India).The language is also taught in Indian schools in the Middle East.

## Dialects

Variations in [intonation](/wiki/Intonation_(linguistics) "Intonation (linguistics)") patterns, vocabulary, and distribution of grammatical and [phonological](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") elements are observable along the parameters of region, religion, community, occupation, social stratum, style and register.

According to the Dravidian Encyclopedia, the regional [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") of Malayalam can be divided into fifteen dialect areas.[[7]](#cite_note-Dravidian_Encyclopedia-7) They are as follows:

* [Kasaragod](/wiki/Kasaragod_district "Kasaragod district")
* [North Malabar](/wiki/North_Malabar "North Malabar")
* [Wayanad](/wiki/Wayanad "Wayanad")
* [Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode")
* [Eranad](/wiki/Eranad "Eranad")
* [Valluvanad](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Valluvanad "Kingdom of Valluvanad") ([South Malabar](/wiki/South_Malabar "South Malabar"))
* [Palakkad](/wiki/Palakkad "Palakkad")
* [Thrissur](/wiki/Thrissur "Thrissur")-[Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi")
* North Travancore
* West [Vembanad](/wiki/Vembanad "Vembanad")
* Central [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore")
* South [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore")
* [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep")
* [Beary](/wiki/Beary_language "Beary language")
* [Ravula](/wiki/Ravula_language "Ravula language")

According to Ethnologue, the dialects are:[[35]](#cite_note-ethnologuecom-36) Malabar, Nagari-Malayalam, North Kerala, Central Kerala, South Kerala, Kayavar, [Namboodiri](/wiki/Namboodiri "Namboodiri"), [Nair](/wiki/Nair "Nair"), [Mappila](/wiki/Arabi_Malayalam "Arabi Malayalam"), [Beary](/wiki/Beary_language "Beary language"), [Jeseri](/wiki/Jeseri "Jeseri"), [Yerava](/wiki/Ravula_language "Ravula language"), Pulaya, [Nasrani](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians"), and [Kasargod](/wiki/Kasargod "Kasargod"). The community dialects are: [Namboodiri](/wiki/Nambudiri "Nambudiri"), [Nair](/wiki/Nair "Nair"), [Arabi Malayalam](/wiki/Arabi_Malayalam "Arabi Malayalam"), Pulaya, and [Nasrani](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians").[[35]](#cite_note-ethnologuecom-36) Whereas both the [Namboothiri](/wiki/Nambudiri "Nambudiri") and Nair dialects have a common nature, the [Arabi Malayalam](/wiki/Arabi_Malayalam "Arabi Malayalam") is among the most divergent of dialects, differing considerably from literary Malayalam.[[35]](#cite_note-ethnologuecom-36) [Jeseri](/wiki/Jeseri "Jeseri") is a dialect of Malayalam spoken mainly in the Union territory of [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep") and [Beary](/wiki/Beary_language "Beary language") is spoken in [Tulu Nadu](/wiki/Tulu_Nadu "Tulu Nadu") which are nearer to Kerala. Of the total 33,066,392 Malayalam speakers in India in 2001, 33,015,420 spoke the standard dialects, 19,643 spoke the [*Yerava*](/wiki/Ravula_language "Ravula language") dialect and 31,329 spoke non-standard regional variations like *[Eranadan](/wiki/Aranadan_language "Aranadan language")*.[[80]](#cite_note-83)

The dialects of Malayalam spoken in the districts like [Kasaragod](/wiki/Kasaragod_district "Kasaragod district"), [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur_district "Kannur district"), [Wayanad](/wiki/Wayanad "Wayanad"), [Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode"), and [Malappuram](/wiki/Malappuram_district "Malappuram district") in the former [Malabar District](/wiki/Malabar_District "Malabar District") have few influences from [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada").[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37) For example, the words those start with the sound "V" in Malayalam become "B" in these districts as in [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada").[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37) Also the [Voiced retroflex approximant](/wiki/Voiced_retroflex_approximant "Voiced retroflex approximant") (/ɻ/) which is seen in both Tamil and the standard form of Malayalam, are not seen in the northern dialects of Malayalam, as in [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada").[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37) For example, the words *Vazhi* (Path), *Vili* (Call), *Vere* (Another), and *Vaa* (Come/Mouth), become *Bayi*, *Bili*, *Bere*, and *Baa* in the northern dialects of Malayalam.[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37) Similarly the Malayalam spoken in the southern districts of Kerala, i.e., [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_district "Thiruvananthapuram district")-[Kollam](/wiki/Kollam_district "Kollam district")-[Pathanamthitta](/wiki/Pathanamthitta_district "Pathanamthitta district") area is influenced by Tamil.[[36]](#cite_note-sreedhara_menon-2007-37)

Labels such as "Nampoothiri Dialect", "Mappila Dialect", and "Nasrani Dialect" refer to overall patterns constituted by the sub-dialects spoken by the subcastes or sub-groups of each such caste. The most outstanding features of the major communal dialects of Malayalam are summarized below:

* Lexical items with phonological features reminiscent of Sanskrit (e.g., *viddhi* meaning 'fool'), *bhosku* 'lie', *musku* 'impudence', *dustu* 'impurity', and *eebhyan* and *sumbhan* (both meaning 'good-for-nothing fellow') abound in Nampoothiri dialect.[[81]](#cite_note-84)
* The Muslim dialect, also known as [Arabi Malayalam](/wiki/Arabi_Malayalam "Arabi Malayalam"), shows maximum divergence from the literary Standard Dialect of Malayalam. It is very much influenced by [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") and [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") rather than by Sanskrit or by English. The retroflex continuant *zha* of the literary dialect is realised in the Muslim dialect as the palatal *ya*. In some other dialects of Northern Kerala too, *zha* of the literary dialect is realised as *ya*.
* The Syrian Christian or Nasrani dialect of Malayalam is quite close to the [Nair](/wiki/Nair "Nair") dialect, especially in [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology"). The speech of the educated section among Syrian Christians and that of those who are close to the church are peculiar in having a number of assimilated as well as unassimilated loan words from [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language"). The few loan words which have found their way into the Christian dialect are assimilated in many cases through the process of de-aspiration.[[82]](#cite_note-85)[[83]](#cite_note-A_sacred_language_is_vanishing-86)
* The [Ravula](/wiki/Ravula_language "Ravula language") is a tribal dialect of Malayalam spoken by the members of [Ravula](/wiki/Ravula "Ravula"), an indigenous people's tribe who are primarily inhabitants of the [Kodagu district](/wiki/Kodagu_district "Kodagu district") of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka").[[84]](#cite_note-87)
* Tamil spoken in the [Kanyakumari district](/wiki/Kanyakumari_district "Kanyakumari district") has influences from Malayalam language.[[85]](#cite_note-88)

### External influences and loanwords

Malayalam has incorporated many elements from other languages over the years, the most notable of these being [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") and later, English.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherKumari1997xxiv,_xxv-89) According to [Sooranad Kunjan Pillai](/wiki/Sooranad_Kunjan_Pillai "Sooranad Kunjan Pillai") who compiled the authoritative Malayalam lexicon, the other principal languages whose vocabulary was incorporated over the ages were [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"), [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language"), [Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali"), [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit"), and [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language").[[87]](#cite_note-90)

* Many medieval [liturgical](/wiki/Liturgical "Liturgical") texts were written in an admixture of [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") and early Malayalam, called [Manipravalam](/wiki/Manipravalam "Manipravalam").[[88]](#cite_note-91) The influence of [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") was very prominent in formal Malayalam used in the medieval literature. Malayalam has a substantially high number of Sanskrit loanwords but these are seldom used.[[89]](#cite_note-92)
* Some [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") loanwords like *adālattŭ* (court of justice), *jāmyaṃ* (bail), *japti* (foreclosure), *jilla* (district), *tālukkŭ* (subdistrict), etc., are used in the formal literary Malayalam for administrative purposes.
* Loanwords and influences also from [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"), [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language"), and [Ladino](/wiki/Ladino_language "Ladino language") abound in the [Jewish Malayalam dialects](/wiki/Judeo-Malayalam "Judeo-Malayalam"), as well as [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language"), and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") in the Christian dialects, while [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") and Persian elements predominate in the [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") dialects.
* The Muslim dialect known as [Mappila Malayalam](/wiki/Mappila_Malayalam "Mappila Malayalam") is predominantly in the northern districts of Kerala. Another Muslim dialect called [Beary bashe](/wiki/Beary_bashe "Beary bashe") is used in the extreme northern part of Kerala along with the southern part of Karnataka in a former region called [Tulu Nadu](/wiki/Tulu_Nadu "Tulu Nadu").

## Geographic distribution and population

See also: [Kerala Gulf diaspora](/wiki/Kerala_Gulf_diaspora "Kerala Gulf diaspora") and [States of India by Malayalam speakers](/wiki/States_of_India_by_Malayalam_speakers "States of India by Malayalam speakers")

| State/Union Territory | Malayalam speakers 2011[[5]](#cite_note-mw-5) | Proportion |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") | 32,413,213 | 97.03% |
| [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep") | 54,264 | 84.17% |
| [Andaman and Nicobar Islands](/wiki/Andaman_and_Nicobar_Islands "Andaman and Nicobar Islands") | 27,475 | 7.22% |
| [Puducherry](/wiki/Puducherry_(union_territory) "Puducherry (union territory)") | 47,973 | 3.84% |
| [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka") | 7,74,057 | 2.22% |
| [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu") | 7,26,096 | 2.08% |
| **[India](/wiki/India "India")** | **34,838,819** | **2.88%** |

Malayalam is a language spoken by the native people of southwestern India and the islands of [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep") in the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea"). According to the Indian census of 2011, there were 32,413,213 speakers of Malayalam in Kerala, making up 93.2% of the total number of Malayalam speakers in India, and 97.03% of the total population of the state. There were a further 7,74,057 (2.22% of the total number) in [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), 7,26,096 (2.05%) in [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu"), and 406,358 (1.2%) in [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra").

The number of Malayalam speakers in [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep") is 51,100, which is only 0.15% of the total number, but is as much as about 84% of the population of Lakshadweep. Malayalam was the most spoken language in erstwhile [Gudalur taluk](/wiki/Gudalur_taluk "Gudalur taluk") (now Gudalur and Panthalur taluks) of [Nilgiris district](/wiki/Nilgiris_district "Nilgiris district") in Tamil Nadu which accounts for 48.8% population and it was the second most spoken language in [Mangalore](/wiki/Mangalore "Mangalore") and Puttur taluks of [South Canara](/wiki/South_Canara "South Canara") accounting for 21.2% and 15.4% respectively according to 1951 census report.[[90]](#cite_note-93) 25.57% of the total population in the [Kodagu district](/wiki/Kodagu_district "Kodagu district") of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka") are [Malayalis](/wiki/Malayali "Malayali"), and they form the single largest linguistic group accounting for 35.5% in the [Virajpet](/wiki/Virajpet "Virajpet") Taluk.[[91]](#cite_note-censusindiagovin-94) Around one-third of the [Malayalis](/wiki/Malayali "Malayali") in [Kodagu district](/wiki/Kodagu_district "Kodagu district") speak the [Yerava dialect](/wiki/Ravula_language "Ravula language") according to the 2011 census, which is native to Kodagu and [Wayanad](/wiki/Wayanad "Wayanad").[[91]](#cite_note-censusindiagovin-94)

In all, Malayalis made up 3.22% of the total Indian population in 2011. Of the total 34,713,130 Malayalam speakers in India in 2011, 33,015,420 spoke the standard dialects, 19,643 spoke the *Yerava* dialect and 31,329 spoke non-standard regional variations like *Eranadan*.[[92]](#cite_note-95) As per the 1991 census data, 28.85% of all Malayalam speakers in India spoke a second language and 19.64% of the total knew three or more languages.

Just before independence, [Malaya](/wiki/British_Malaya "British Malaya") attracted many Malayalis. Large numbers of Malayalis have settled in [Chennai](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai"), [Bengaluru](/wiki/Bengaluru "Bengaluru"), [Mangaluru](/wiki/Mangaluru "Mangaluru"), [Hyderabad](/wiki/Hyderabad,_Telangana "Hyderabad, Telangana"), [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"), [Navi Mumbai](/wiki/Navi_Mumbai "Navi Mumbai"), [Pune](/wiki/Pune "Pune"), [Mysuru](/wiki/Mysuru "Mysuru") and [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"). Many Malayalis have also emigrated to the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), the United States, and Europe. There were 179,860 speakers of Malayalam in the United States, according to the 2000 census, with the highest concentrations in [Bergen County, New Jersey](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey"), and [Rockland County, New York](/wiki/Rockland_County,_New_York "Rockland County, New York").[[93]](#cite_note-96) There are 144,000 of Malayalam speakers in [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] There were 11,687 Malayalam speakers in Australia in 2016.[[94]](#cite_note-97) The 2001 [Canadian census](/wiki/Canadian_census "Canadian census") reported 7,070 people who listed Malayalam as their mother tongue, mainly in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"). The 2006 New Zealand census reported 2,139 speakers.[[95]](#cite_note-98) 134 Malayalam speaking households were reported in 1956 in [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"). There is also a considerable [Malayali](/wiki/Malayali "Malayali") population in the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") regions, especially in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai"), [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait") and [Doha](/wiki/Doha "Doha").

## Phonology

Main article: [Malayalam phonology](/wiki/Malayalam_phonology "Malayalam phonology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Malayalam_vowels.png/330px-Malayalam_vowels.png)](/wiki/File:Malayalam_vowels.png)

[Monophthongs](/wiki/Monophthong "Monophthong") of Malayalam, from Namboodiripad, Savithry (2016)[[96]](#cite_note-namboodiripad-2016-99)



Literary Malayalam

For the consonants and vowels, the [International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") (IPA) symbol is given, followed by the Malayalam character and the [ISO 15919](/wiki/ISO_15919 "ISO 15919") transliteration.[[97]](#cite_note-haowen_jiang-2010-100)

## Sample text

The following text is Article 1 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights").

### English

All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood.

### Malayalam

മനുഷ്യരെല്ലാവരും തുല്യാവകാശങ്ങളോടും അന്തസ്സോടും സ്വാതന്ത്ര്യത്തോടുംകൂടി ജനിച്ചിട്ടുള്ളവരാണ്‌. അന്യോന്യം ഭ്രാതൃഭാവത്തോടെ പെരുമാറുവാനാണ്‌ മനുഷ്യന് വിവേകബുദ്ധിയും മനസാക്ഷിയും സിദ്ധമായിരിക്കുന്നത്‌.

### Romanisation (ISO 15919)

manuṣyarellāvaruṁ tulyāvakāśaṅṅaḷōṭuṁ antassōṭuṁ svātantryattōṭuṅkūṭi janicciṭṭuḷḷavarāṇ‌ŭ. anyōnyaṁ bhrātr̥bhāvattōṭe perumāṟuvānāṇ‌ŭ manuṣyanŭ vivēkabuddhiyuṁ manasākṣiyuṁ siddhamāyirikkunnat‌ŭ.

### IPA

/manuʂjaɾellaːʋaɾum t̪uljaːʋakaːʃaŋŋaɭoːʈum an̪t̪assoːʈum sʋaːt̪an̪t̪rjat̪t̪oːʈuŋkuːʈi d͡ʒanit͡ʃt͡ʃiʈʈuɭɭaʋaɾaːɳɨ̆ ǁ anjoːnjam bʱraːt̪rɨ̆bʱaːʋat̪t̪oːʈe peɾumaːruʋaːnaːɳɨ̆ manuʂjanɨ̆ ʋiʋeːkabud̪d̪ʱijum manasaːkʂijum sid̪d̪ʱamaːjiɾikkun̪ːat̪ɨ̆ ǁ/

## Grammar

Main article: [Malayalam grammar](/wiki/Malayalam_grammar "Malayalam grammar")

Malayalam has a canonical word order of [SOV](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93object%E2%80%93verb "Subject–object–verb") (subject–object–verb), as do other [Dravidian languages](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages").[[98]](#cite_note-104) A rare [OSV](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93object%E2%80%93verb "Subject–object–verb") word order occurs in interrogative clauses when the interrogative word is the subject.[[99]](#cite_note-105) Both [adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") and [possessive adjectives](/wiki/Possessive_adjective "Possessive adjective") precede the [nouns](/wiki/Noun "Noun") they modify. Malayalam has 6[[100]](#cite_note-106) or 7[[101]](#cite_note-107)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] [grammatical cases](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case"). Verbs are conjugated for tense, mood and aspect, but not for person, gender nor number except in archaic or poetic language. The modern Malayalam grammar is based on the book *[Kerala Panineeyam](/wiki/Kerala_Panineeyam "Kerala Panineeyam")* written by [A. R. Raja Raja Varma](/wiki/A._R._Raja_Raja_Varma "A. R. Raja Raja Varma") in late 19th century CE.[[25]](#cite_note-Chandran_2018-26)

### Nouns

The [declensional](/wiki/Declension "Declension") paradigms for some common nouns and pronouns are given below. As Malayalam is an agglutinative language, it is difficult to delineate the cases strictly and determine how many there are, although seven or eight is the generally accepted number. [Alveolar plosives](/wiki/Alveolar_plosive "Alveolar plosive") and [nasals](/wiki/Alveolar_nasal "Alveolar nasal") (although the modern [Malayalam script](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script") does not distinguish the latter from the [dental nasal](/wiki/Dental_nasal "Dental nasal")) are underlined for clarity, following the convention of the [National Library at Kolkata romanization](/wiki/National_Library_at_Kolkata_romanization "National Library at Kolkata romanization").

### Words adopted from Sanskrit

When words are adopted from Sanskrit, their endings are usually changed to conform to Malayalam norms:

#### Nouns

* Masculine [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") nouns with a [word stem](/wiki/Word_stem "Word stem") ending in a short /a/ take the ending /an/ in the nominative singular. For example, [Kr̥ṣṇa](/wiki/Krishna "Krishna") → Kr̥ṣṇan. The final /n/ is dropped before masculine surnames, honorifics, or titles ending in /an/ and beginning with a consonant other than /n/ – e.g., "Krishna Menon", "Krishna Kaniyaan" etc., but "Krishnan Ezhutthachan". Surnames ending with /ar/ or /aḷ/ (where these are plural forms of "an" denoting respect) are treated similarly – "Krishna Pothuval", "Krishna Chakyar", but "Krishnan Nair", "Krishnan Nambiar", as are Sanskrit surnames such "Varma(n)", "Sharma(n)", or "Gupta(n)" (rare) – e.g., "Krishna Varma", "Krishna Sharman". If a name is a compound, only the last element undergoes this transformation – e.g., "Kr̥ṣṇa" + "dēva" = "Kr̥ṣṇadēvan", not "Kr̥ṣṇandēvan".
* Feminine words ending in a long /ā/ or /ī/ are changed to end in a short /a/ or /i/, for example "[Sītā](/wiki/Sita "Sita")" → "Sīta" and "[Lakṣmī](/wiki/Lakshmi "Lakshmi")" → "Lakṣmi". However, the long vowel still appears in compound words, such as "Sītādēvi" or" Lakṣmīdēvi". The long ī is generally reserved for the [vocative](/wiki/Vocative_case "Vocative case") forms of these names, although in Sanskrit the vocative actually takes a short /i/. There are also a small number of nominative /ī/ endings that have not been shortened – a prominent example being the word "strī" for "woman".
* Nouns that have a stem in /-an/ and which end with a long /ā/ in the masculine nominative singular have /vŭ/ added to them, for example "[Brahmā](/wiki/Brahm%C4%81 "Brahmā")" (stem "Brahman") → "Brahmāvŭ". When the same nouns are declined in the neuter and take a short /a/ ending in Sanskrit, Malayalam adds an additional /m/, e.g. "Brahma" (neuter nominative singular of "[Brahman](/wiki/Brahman "Brahman")") becomes "Brahmam". This is again omitted when forming compounds.
* Words whose roots end in /-an/ but whose nominative singular ending is /-a-/ (for example, the Sanskrit root of "[karma](/wiki/Karma "Karma")" is actually "karman") are also changed. The original root is ignored and "karma" (the form in Malayalam being "karmam" because it ends in a short /a/) is taken as the basic form of the noun when declining.[[102]](#cite_note-110) However, this does not apply to all consonant stems, as "unchangeable" stems such as "manas" ("mind") and "suhr̥t" ("friend") are identical to the Malayalam nominative singular forms (although the regularly derived "manam" sometimes occurs as an alternative to "manas").
* Sanskrit words describing things or animals rather than people with a stem in short /a/ end with an /m/ in Malayalam. For example, "[Rāmāyaṇa](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana")" → "Rāmāyaṇam". In most cases, this is actually the same as the Sanskrit accusative case ending, which is also /m/ (or, allophonically, anusvara due to the requirements of the [sandhi](/wiki/Sandhi "Sandhi") word-combining rules) in the neuter nominative. However, "things and animals" and "people" are not always differentiated based on whether or not they are sentient beings; for example, "[Narasimha](/wiki/Narasimha "Narasimha")" becomes "Narasiṃham" and not "Narasiṃhan", whereas "[Ananta](/wiki/Ananta_Shesha "Ananta Shesha")" becomes "Anantan" even though both are sentient. This does not strictly correspond to the Sanskrit neuter gender, as both "Narasiṃha" and "Ananta" are masculine nouns in the original Sanskrit.
* Nouns with short vowel stems other than /a/, such as "[Viṣṇu](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu")", "[Prajāpati](/wiki/Prajapati "Prajapati")" etc. are declined with the Sanskrit stem acting as the Malayalam nominative singular (the Sanskrit nominative singular is formed by adding a visarga, e.g., as in "Viṣṇuḥ")
* The original Sanskrit vocative is often used in formal or poetic Malayalam, e.g. "Harē" (for "[Hari](/wiki/Hari "Hari")") or "Prabhō" (for "Prabhu" – "Lord"). This is restricted to certain contexts – mainly when addressing deities or other exalted individuals, so a normal man named Hari would usually be addressed using a Malayalam vocative such as "Harī". The Sanskrit genitive is also occasionally found in Malayalam poetry, especially the personal pronouns "mama" ("my" or "mine") and "tava" ("thy" or "thine"). Other cases are less common and generally restricted to the realm of Maṇipravāḷam.
* Along with these [tatsama](/wiki/Tatsama "Tatsama") borrowings, there are also many [tadbhava](/wiki/Tadbhava "Tadbhava") words in common use. These were incorporated via borrowing before the separation of Malayalam and Tamil. As the language did not then accommodate Sanskrit phonology as it now does, words were changed to conform to the Old Tamil phonological system, for example "Kr̥ṣṇa" → "Kaṇṇan".[[103]](#cite_note-111) Most of his works are oriented on the basic Malayalam family and cultures and many of them were path-breaking in the history of Malayalam literature

## Writing system

Main articles: [Malayalam script](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script") and [Malayalam braille](/wiki/Malayalam_braille "Malayalam braille")

Aside from the Malayalam script, the Malayalam language has been written in other scripts like [Latin](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"), [Syriac](/wiki/Suriyani_Malayalam "Suriyani Malayalam")[[104]](#cite_note-Suriyani_Malayalam_Archived_11_June_2014_at_the_Wayback_Machine-112)[[83]](#cite_note-A_sacred_language_is_vanishing-86)[[105]](#cite_note-Prayer_from_the_Past_Archived_4_April_2014_at_the_Wayback_Machine-113) and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabi_Malayalam "Arabi Malayalam"). [Suriyani Malayalam](/wiki/Suriyani_Malayalam "Suriyani Malayalam") was used by [Saint Thomas Christians](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians") (also known as Nasranis) until the 19th century.[[104]](#cite_note-Suriyani_Malayalam_Archived_11_June_2014_at_the_Wayback_Machine-112)[[83]](#cite_note-A_sacred_language_is_vanishing-86) Arabic scripts particularly were taught in [madrasahs](/wiki/Madrasah "Madrasah") in Kerala and the [Lakshadweep Islands](/wiki/Lakshadweep_Islands "Lakshadweep Islands").[[106]](#cite_note-114)[[107]](#cite_note-115)

### Malayalam script

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/St_angelo_fort_Arakkal_Museum.JPG/250px-St_angelo_fort_Arakkal_Museum.JPG)](/wiki/File:St_angelo_fort_Arakkal_Museum.JPG)

A Malayalam signboard from [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur "Kannur"), Kerala. Malayalam is official language in the [Indian](/wiki/India "India") state of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") and the union territories of [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep") and [Puduchery](/wiki/Puducherry_(union_territory) "Puducherry (union territory)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Malayalam_board_with_old_style_Malayalam_letter_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Malayalam_board_with_old_style_Malayalam_letter_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malayalam_board_with_old_style_Malayalam_letter_(cropped).jpg)

A Board in Malayalam which uses the complex letters in traditional script

Historically, several scripts were used to write Malayalam. Among these were the Vatteluttu, [Kolezhuthu](/wiki/Kolezhuthu "Kolezhuthu") and [Malayanma](/wiki/Malayanma "Malayanma") scripts. But it was the [Grantha script](/wiki/Grantha_script "Grantha script"), another [Southern Brahmi](/wiki/Southern_Brahmi "Southern Brahmi") variation, which gave rise to the modern [Malayalam script](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script"). The modern Malayalam script bears high similarity to [Tigalari script](/wiki/Tigalari_script "Tigalari script"), which was used for writing [Tulu language](/wiki/Tulu_language "Tulu language") in [Coastal Karnataka](/wiki/South_Canara "South Canara") ([Dakshina Kannada](/wiki/Dakshina_Kannada "Dakshina Kannada") and [Udupi](/wiki/Udupi_district "Udupi district") districts) and the northernmost [Kasaragod district](/wiki/Kasaragod_district "Kasaragod district") of Kerala.[[24]](#cite_note-vinodh_rajan-25) It is syllabic in the sense that the sequence of graphic elements means that syllables have to be read as units, though in this system the elements representing individual vowels and consonants are for the most part readily identifiable. In the 1960s Malayalam dispensed with many special letters representing less frequent conjunct consonants and combinations of the vowel /u, u:/ with different consonants.

Malayalam script consists of a total of 578 characters. The script contains 52 letters including 16 vowels and 36 consonants, which forms 576 syllabic characters, and contains two additional diacritic characters named [anusvāra](/wiki/Anusv%C4%81ra "Anusvāra") and [visarga](/wiki/Visarga "Visarga").[[108]](#cite_note-116)[[109]](#cite_note-117) The earlier style of writing has been superseded by a new style as of 1981. This new script reduces the different letters for typesetting from 900 to fewer than 90. This was mainly done to include Malayalam in the keyboards of typewriters and computers.

In 1999 a group named "Rachana Akshara Vedi" produced a set of free [fonts](/wiki/Typeface "Typeface") containing the entire character repertoire of more than 900 [glyphs](/wiki/Glyph "Glyph"). This was announced and released along with a [text editor](/wiki/Text_editor "Text editor") in the same year at [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram"), the capital of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"). In 2004, the fonts were released under the [GPL](/wiki/GNU_General_Public_License "GNU General Public License") license by [Richard Stallman](/wiki/Richard_Stallman "Richard Stallman").[[110]](#cite_note-118) A dedicated Malayalam operating system was released in 2006.[[111]](#cite_note-119)

### Chillu letters

A *chillu* (ചില്ല്, *cillŭ*), or a *chillaksharam* (ചില്ലക്ഷരം, *cillakṣaram*), is a special consonant letter that represents a pure consonant independently, without help of a [virama](/wiki/Virama "Virama"). Unlike a consonant represented by an ordinary consonant letter, this consonant is never followed by an inherent vowel. [Anusvara](#Anusvaram) and [visarga](#Visargam) fit this definition but are not usually included. ISCII and Unicode 5.0 treat a *chillu* as a glyph variant of a normal ("base") consonant letter.[[112]](#cite_note-120) In Unicode 5.1 and later, *chillu* letters are treated as independent characters, encoded atomically.

*Chillu* letters

| Letter | Unicode name | Base | Remarks | Examples |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ൺ | CHILLU NN | *ṇa* **ണ** |  | കൂൺ (kūṇ, "mushroom") |
| ൻ | CHILLU N | *ṉa* **ന** | Chillu of dental – alveolar nasal *ṉa*. | അവൻ (avaṉ, "he") |
| ർ | CHILLU RR | *ṟa* **റ** | Historically stood for *ra* ര, not *ṟa* റ. | അവർ (avar̠, "they") |
| ൽ | CHILLU L | *la* **ല** |  | കാൽ (kāl, "foot") |
| ൾ | CHILLU LL | *ḷa* **ള** |  | അവൾ (avaḷ, "she") |
| ൿ | CHILLU K | *ka* **ക** | Not in modern use | വാൿചാതുരി (doesn't occur word finally.) |
| ൔ | CHILLU M | *ma* **മ** | Not in modern use |  |
| ൕ | CHILLU Y | *ya* **യ** | Not in modern use |  |
| ൖ | CHILLU LLL | *ḻa* **ഴ** | Not in modern use |  |

### Number system and other symbols

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Praślēṣam | ഽ | Corresponds to [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") [avagraha](/wiki/Avagraha "Avagraha"), used when a Sanskrit phrase containing an *avagraha* is written in Malayalam script. The symbol indicates the [elision](/wiki/Elision "Elision") of the word-initial vowel *a* after a word that ends in *ā*, *ē*, or *ō*, and is transliterated as an apostrophe ('), or sometimes as a colon + an apostrophe (:'). ([Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam_language "Malayalam language"): പ്രശ്ലേഷം, *praślēṣam*) |
